name: celerite2 (Scalable GP Regression for Time Series)
pricing_notes: Open source (MIT); Dan Foreman-Mackey / Flatiron Institute

performance.notes: O(n log n) instead of O(n^3); C++ extension; handles 1M+ points

agent_readiness.notes: Scalable GP regression for unevenly sampled time series. import celerite2. from celerite2 import terms. kernel = terms.SHOTerm(S0=1.0, Q=1.0, w0=1.0). gp = celerite2.GaussianProcess(kernel, mean=0.0). gp.compute(x, yerr=yerr). ll = gp.log_likelihood(y). mu, var = gp.predict(y, x_pred, return_var=True). PyMC: celerite2.pymc. JAX: celerite2.jax. NumPy default. O(n) scaling for semi-separable covariances. Astronomy time series (Kepler, TESS stellar variability). Quasi-periodic oscillations.

gotchas: Only works with specific kernel families (SHO, Matern32, RotationTerm, etc.) — not arbitrary kernels
Strictly for 1D input (time series) — not general GP regression
Kernel parameter interpretation requires understanding oscillator physics
celerite2 (not celerite) is the modern version — avoid legacy celerite
best_when: Astronomy light curve modeling with GP noise (Kepler/TESS stellar variability)
Scalable GP regression for unevenly sampled time series with 1M+ points
When O(n^3) standard GP is too slow for long time series
avoid_when: Arbitrary kernel functions (only semi-separable kernels supported)
summary: celerite2 provides O(n) scalable GP regression for unevenly sampled time series. terms.SHOTerm + GaussianProcess(kernel). gp.compute(x, yerr).log_likelihood(y). gp.predict(). PyMC and JAX backends. MIT by Dan Foreman-Mackey. Handles 1M+ point time series where standard GPs fail. Limited to semi-separable kernels (SHO, Matern32, RotationTerm). Best for astronomy light curves and quasi-periodic oscillation modeling.
